figures.caption: Figure 4. Asymmetric per-instance impact of caption errors on Qwen2.5-VL-3B (100 image–question instances): fabrication shifts predictions sharply (∆p = 0.64, 87% flips), while omission is mild on average (∆p = 0.13) but its long tail still flips 11% of answers. ∆p is the caption-induced shift toward the wrong answer; answers flip above the shaded threshold (∆p > 0.4). Inner boxes mark median and IQR; diamonds mark means.

Table 1. Effect of self-generated captions on HallusionBench (Qwen2.5-VL-3B†). Naively embedding a self-generated caption degrades performance by nearly 10 points, an early indication of the anchoring effect analyzed below.

Figure 5. Overview of the GEASS pipeline. Given an image I and a question Q, the model first generates a caption C via self-captioning (Stage 1). Two parallel forward passes through the same VLM with shared parameters produce logit vectors zclean (conditioned on I,Q) and zcap (conditioned on I,Q,C) (Stage 2). The adaptive fusion module (Stage 3) computes a confidence gate α that assesses whether the model needs help, and an information gain weight w that evaluates whether the caption can provide it, yielding the effective fusion weight αeff = α · w. The final logit zfinal is decoded into the output answer (Stage 4).

Figure 1. Caption anchoring effect observed on Qwen2.5-VL-3B with chain-of-thought reasoning. The model’s output (left, in red) closely mirrors the phrasing of the embedded caption (right, in red), demonstrating that captions reshape not only the final answer but the model’s entire reasoning trajectory.

Table 2. N-gram overlap (%) between model output and embedded caption text, averaged over 100 HallusionBench samples. † denotes reasoning-augmented variant. All four models exhibit a sharp rise in overlap when the caption is embedded, with bigram and trigram increases consistently larger than unigram increases—the signature of caption-induced anchoring.
Figure 2. Left: Objects contained in the caption generated by InternVL2-8B. Right: Salient objects that are clearly visible in the image but not mentioned in the caption.

Figure 3. Top: With a correct caption mentioning “a dog sitting on the beach,” both Qwen2.5-VL-3B and InternVL2-8B revise their initially incorrect answers from No to Yes, demonstrating confidence grounding. Bottom: With a wrong caption mentioning “a cat sitting on the beach,” both models similarly flip to Yes and fabricate supporting details, demonstrating hallucination amplification. The same anchoring mechanism produces opposite outcomes depending on caption accuracy.
